benchmark_name: Benchmarking joint multi-omics dimensionality reduction approaches for the study of cancer
dataset: Simulated multi-omics datasets, TCGA cancer multi-omics datasets, and multi-omics single-cell cancer cell line data
metric: Adjusted Rand Index; C-index; log-rank p-value; enrichment score
metric_definition: Clustering agreement, survival association, clinical annotation association, and biological pathway/process enrichment for joint dimensionality-reduction factors
rank_scope: joint dimensionality reduction benchmark; bounded factor interpretability support, not overall-best ranking
result_text: In this benchmark, MOFA should not be described as the overall best clustering method; intNMF is strongest for sample clustering and MCIA is broadly robust. MOFA is supported as useful for sparse, biologically interpretable latent factors and for identifying clinically or survival-associated molecular features across multi-omics layers.
claim_span: Abstract plus Results sections for simulated clustering, TCGA survival/clinical/biological annotations, and Fig. 3/Fig. 4; use as bounded factor-interpretability evidence rather than global superiority evidence.
evaluation_protocol: Systematic benchmark of nine joint dimensionality-reduction methods on simulated multi-omics data, TCGA cancer multi-omics data, and single-cell multi-omics cancer cell line data
paper_title: Benchmarking joint multi-omics dimensionality reduction approaches for the study of cancer
notes: Human-verified correction of benchmark-gap source; corrected DOI from earlier ambiguous/user-supplied DOI to 10.1038/s41467-020-20430-7; qualitative benchmark conclusion only; no numeric rank/score invented; evidence supports bounded factor-interpretability use, not universal superiority.

dataset: Human embryonic forebrain data, simulated data, and controlled RNA velocity workflow analyses
metric: Cross-boundary direction correctness; phase portrait distortion; k-nearest-neighbor smoothing sensitivity
metric_definition: Assessment of whether velocity outputs recapitulate known differentiation direction and remain robust to modeling, smoothing, and embedding assumptions
rank_scope: RNA velocity theoretical/empirical critique; caveat evidence
result_text: scVelo remains the canonical dynamic-model RNA velocity tool, but this critique shows that its outputs can fail or even reverse known trajectories under some conditions. Recommendation reports must include caveats about sensitivity to k-nearest-neighbor smoothing, embedding choices, and transcription/splicing/degradation model assumptions.
claim_span: Author Summary and Results discussion of human embryonic forebrain direction reversal, informal theoretical foundations, and workflow sensitivity; use as caveat/negative-control evidence, not positive ranking evidence.
evaluation_protocol: Mathematical analysis and controlled empirical evaluation of velocyto/scVelo RNA velocity assumptions, workflow sensitivity, and known differentiation-direction consistency
notes: Human-verified caveat benchmark fact; use as recommendation safety evidence, not as a positive performance ranking; no numeric score invented; semantic auditor should block strong uncaveated scVelo performance claims.
